annotator S.report_text:
Seaduke

The malware hides behind numerous layers of encryption and obfuscation Adversaries may attempt to make an executable or file difficult to discover or analyze by encrypting, encoding, or otherwise obfuscating its contents on the system or in transit.

is capable of quietly stealing and exfiltrating sensitive information such as email from the victim's computer. Adversaries may target email on local systems to collect sensitive information.
Adversaries may steal data by exfiltrating it over an alternative protocol.

In the months that followed, the Duke group began to target victims with "Office Monkeys"- and "eFax"-themed emails, booby-trapped with a Cozyduke payload. Adversaries may send phishing messages to gain access to victim systems.

Cozyduke

Miniduke

Cosmicduke

The attackers control Cozyduke via compromised websites, issuing instructions to infected machines by uploading "tasks" to a database file. This technique describes the use of web services for command and control malware.

One such task (an encoded PowerShell script) instructed Cozyduke to download and execute Seaduke from a compromised website. Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands and scripts.

Seaduke securely communicates with the C&C server over HTTP/HTTPS Adversaries may communicate using web protocols to avoid detection/network filtering by blending in with existing traffic.

beneath layers of encoding (Base64) and encryption (RC4, AES). Adversaries may employ an encryption algorithm to conceal command and control traffic rather than relying on any inherent protections provided by a communication protocol.

They have the ability to retrieve detailed bot/system information, Gathering local information from the compromised system, such as operating system details, network configurations, and other information relevant to malware operations.

Impersonation using Kerberos pass-the-ticket attacks (Mimikatz PowerShell) Adversaries use stolen Kerberos tickets to authenticate to systems using Pass-the-Ticket (PtT).

Email extraction from the MS Exchange Server using compromised credentials Adversaries may target email on local systems to collect sensitive information.

Archiving sensitive information Adversaries may compress and/or encrypt data collected prior to exfiltration to reduce the size and/or evade detection.

Data exfiltration via legitimate cloud services Adversaries may exfiltrate data via direct connections to cloud storage services.

Secure file deletion Adversaries may wipe or corrupt raw disk data on specific systems or in large numbers in a network to interrupt availability to system and network resources.

Adversaries may attempt to access credential material stored in the process memory of the LSASS.

Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts

annotator L.report_text:
Seaduke

The malware hides behind numerous layers of encryption and obfuscation Threat actors use various obfuscation and encryption methods to make it difficult to detect and analyze their tools and techniques.

is capable of quietly stealing and exfiltrating sensitive information such as email from the victim's computer. Seaduke is able to exfiltrate sensitive information such as emails from the victim's machine.

Seaduke victims are generally first infected with Cozyduke and, if the computer appears to be a target of interest, the operators will install Seaduke

Cozyduke

Miniduke

Cosmicduke

Cozy Bear

the Duke group began to target victims with "Office Monkeys"- and "eFax"-themed emails, booby-trapped with a Cozyduke payload themed emails were sent with a CozyDuke payload

The attackers control Cozyduke via compromised websites, issuing instructions to infected machines by uploading "tasks" to a database file Attackers control Cozyduke through compromised Web sites by uploading "tasks" to a database file. This technique describes the use of web services for command and control malware.

One such task (an encoded PowerShell script) instructed Cozyduke to download and execute Seaduke from a compromised website A task described as a coded PowerShell script that instructs Cozyduke to download and run Seaduke from a compromised website

Seaduke securely communicates with the C&C server over HTTP/HTTPS Use of HTTP/HTTPS as an application layer protocol for communication with the C&C server.

They have the ability to retrieve detailed bot/system information Gathering local information from the compromised system, such as operating system details, network configurations, and other information relevant to malware operations.

upload files, download files Uploading and downloading files between the compromised system and the C&C server. This may include updating bot configuration, uploading command or script files, and downloading additional payloads.

self-delete the malware from the system Ability to self-destruct malware from the compromised system. This can help avoid detection and remove traces of the infection to maintain persistent access.

Impersonation using Kerberos pass-the-ticket attacks (Mimikatz PowerShell) Using Kerberos pass-the-ticket attacks to impersonate legitimate users and gain unauthorized access to systems.

Email extraction from the MS Exchange Server using compromised credentials Extracting email from MS Exchange servers using compromised credentials.

Archiving sensitive information Storage of sensitive information collected for exfiltration or later use.

Data exfiltration via legitimate cloud services Data exfiltration through legitimate cloud services to camouflage malicious traffic.

Secure file deletion Secure file deletion to remove traces of malicious activity.

Seaduke securely communicates with the C&C server over HTTP/HTTPS beneath layers of encoding (Base64) and encryption (RC4, AES). encoding and encryption layers were used

Impersonation using Kerberos pass-the-ticket attacks (Mimikatz PowerShell) Mimikatz was used

Email extraction from the MS Exchange Server using compromised credentials compromised credentials were used
